Name: rate-distortion_compression_framework
Title: A Unified Rate-Distortion Framework for Information-Theoretic Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: A structured framework integrating rate-distortion theory with explicit quantization and pruning strategies can achieve optimal model compression while maintaining predictive accuracy across varying tasks and distributions.
Related Work: Existing literature addresses model compression through rate-distortion theory, highlighting the tension between compression ratios and performance (Isik et al., 2021). However, these approaches often lack a unified framework that explicitly connects quantization and pruning strategies with information-theoretic principles and task-relevant distortion measures.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel framework that synthesizes rate-distortion theory with structured model compression, addressing the limitations of existing approaches. By framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we propose a systematic method that derives optimal quantization and pruning strategies based on information-theoretic principles. This framework will explicitly define a distortion measure relevant to the task, enabling a more nuanced allocation of bits for model parameters. We will validate the framework through empirical evaluations across different datasets and tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving optimal compression while maintaining predictive accuracy. Our contributions will include a comprehensive derivation of achievable rates, experimental results against theoretical limits, and a detailed analysis of the trade-offs involved in model compression.
Experiments: 1. Derive achievable rates for various model architectures using the proposed framework and compare them with existing compression methods.
2. Implement quantization strategies based on the derived rates and assess their impact on predictive accuracy across benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
3. Conduct pruning experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in maintaining accuracy while reducing model size, using a well-defined distortion measure.
4. Analyze the relationship between model complexity, rate-distortion trade-offs, and task performance, providing insight into the optimal allocation of bits for different tasks.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of defining an appropriate distortion measure for various tasks, the need for comprehensive empirical evaluations across diverse models, and the possibility that some theoretical predictions may not translate well into practical implementations.
